tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correctly identifying trigeminal neuralgia as the primary diagnosis based on classic paroxysmal facial pain features and appropriate exclusion of key mimics/red flags.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "trigeminal neuralgia" (communication milestone: explain_diagnosis).

Explains typical clinical features: recurrent brief (seconds to ~2 minutes) unilateral electric shock-like facial pain in trigeminal distribution, often triggered by light touch (e.g., brushing teeth, shaving, chewing, wind).
Clarifies that between attacks the neurologic exam is typically normal; asks about sensory loss, persistent pain, or other neurologic deficits as red flags for secondary causes.

Screens for red flags/secondary trigeminal neuralgia: age <40, bilateral symptoms, objective sensory changes, hearing loss/vertigo, ataxia, diplopia, persistent background pain, systemic cancer/infection risk; recommends MRI brain with attention to posterior fossa/trigeminal nerve when red flags are present or to evaluate secondary causes per local practice.

Demonstrates a focused differential (e.g., dental pathology, temporomandibular disorder, sinusitis, migraine/cluster headache, postherpetic neuralgia, giant cell arteritis when age >50 with systemic symptoms, glossopharyngeal neuralgia) and uses history to distinguish.

Provides an initial management overview and includes "carbamazepine" as first-line pharmacotherapy (communication milestone: explain_treatment), while acknowledging that prescribing is not required for this L1 task.

If discussing medication initiation, performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check, contraindication_check, and drug_interaction_check; does not prescribe if any critical safety check is unmet or positive.

Counsels on medication risks at a high level if mentioned (e.g., dizziness/somnolence, hyponatremia, hepatic effects, rash/serious cutaneous reactions) and advises urgent care for severe rash, facial swelling, breathing difficulty, or neurologic deficits.

Provides clear return precautions: new/worsening neurologic deficits, persistent severe headache, fever, vision changes, jaw claudication (if older), or atypical/persistent facial pain warrant urgent evaluation.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose classic trigeminal neuralgia using hallmark pain description and triggers, with brief screening for red flags and key mimics; record diagnosis.
must_collect: paroxysmal duration (seconds to minutes) with pain-free intervals
absence of objective neurologic deficits (or presence as red flag)
age at onset and laterality (bilateral or <40 as red flags)
dental/TMJ symptoms or recent dental work
history of herpes zoster rash or persistent burning pain
headache autonomic features (tearing, rhinorrhea) suggesting cluster/TAC
systemic/vascular red flags if older (vision changes, jaw claudication)
steps: Elicit pain characteristics (quality, duration, frequency) and triggers; localize to trigeminal divisions.
Ask about neurologic symptoms/exam findings and red flags for secondary causes (bilateral, sensory loss, persistent pain, age <40, other neuro deficits).
Briefly consider and rule out common mimics (dental/TMJ, sinusitis, postherpetic neuralgia, migraine/cluster, GCA if appropriate).
Explain diagnosis explicitly as trigeminal neuralgia and provide brief education on the condition.
Mention first-line treatment option carbamazepine as part of treatment explanation (without prescribing in L1).
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis with recommendation for imaging to exclude secondary causes (e.g., MRI) while still recording trigeminal neuralgia as the working diagnosis.
steps: Collect classic trigeminal neuralgia pain features and triggers; assess laterality and distribution.
Screen for secondary-cause red flags and perform/describe a focused neurologic assessment.
Explain trigeminal neuralgia and discuss that MRI may be used to evaluate for secondary causes depending on red flags/local practice.
Mention carbamazepine as first-line medication in the treatment discussion (no prescription required for L1).
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis recorded as trigeminal neuralgia with explicit differential documentation in narrative (without using record_differential tool) and safety-netting.
steps: Elicit pain features consistent with trigeminal neuralgia and identify triggers.
Ask targeted questions to distinguish from dental/TMJ, TAC/cluster, postherpetic neuralgia, migraine, sinusitis, and (when relevant) GCA.
Explain trigeminal neuralgia diagnosis and outline treatment options including carbamazepine; advise follow-up and urgent return precautions for red flags.
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.
